The planned worldwide release has been

items.title: Final Fantasy XIV Mobile to Shut Down as Global Release Is Cancelled

Rumors about a mobile version of Final Fantasy XIV began circulating in 2024. Square Enix later confirmed the project, with a broader release planned following feedback from

items.title: Final Fantasy XIV Mobile Shutting Down in China, Global Release Canceled
items.summary: Tencent and Square Enix have ended their licensing agreement for Final Fantasy XIV Mobile. The Chinese version will shut down and the planned global release has been canceled.
items.content:
Christina Gonzalez Posted: Jul 17, 2026 1:45 PM ET Category: News 0

Tencent and Square Enix have ended their licensing agreement for Final Fantasy XIV Mobile , and the Chinese version of the game, made by Tencent subsidiary Lightspeed Studios, will shut down. This also means that the planned global release won’t be happening.

Today, with the cancelation announcement , they’ve suspended new user registrations and top-ups for the Chinese edition, with shut down set for September 30th. In the announcement, they made it clear that this is the end, and included, “To everyone who has been looking forward to the global version, we also offer our sincere apologies for being unable to meet your expectations”.

Originally announced as a “sister” game and not an actual port of Final Fantasy XIV, the Lightspeed team did collaborate with Naoki Yoshida and flesh out their plans and ambitions for what a FFXIV Mobile could (and should…and shouldn’t) be. These meetings seemed to change Yoshida’s mind about the viability of a mobile venture. They were aiming at FFXIV fans and the casual player, so there was combat and content inspired by the MMORPG, jobs, and challenges, along with key place, starting with A Realm Reborn content, feature the game’s jobs, and had lots of content update plans and additions.

Now, not only will Lightspeed’s plans go unfinished, the project will come to an end soon “Due to adjustments in business operations and changes in the market environment”, which was the reason cited for ending their licensing deal.

Christina is MMORPG.COM’s News Editor and a contributor since 2011. Always a fan of great community and wondering if the same sort of magic that was her first guild exists anymore.

items.title: Final Fantasy XIV Mobile’s Global Launch Officially Cancelled
items.summary: Square Enix and Lightspeed Studios have announced Final Fantasy XIV Mobile is ending service in China, and the game's global launch is cancelled.
items.content:
Square Enix and Lightspeed Studios have announced that Final Fantasy XIV Mobile (Chinese Edition) will end service on September 30, 2026. The game’s worldwide release has also been cancelled.

FFXIV Mobile officially launched in China in June 2025, and while Square initially strongly promoted the game, it slowly stopped talking about it outside of China. Rather than a port of the full game, FFXIV Mobile was more of a standalone MMORPG adaptation meant to condense the story of the main game and provide a more streamlined experience, complete with mobile controls and altered combat. It was primarily developed by Lightspeed Studios, which is owned by Tencent.

This also comes just after Square Enix announced end-of-service for another mobile game, Final Fantasy VII Ever Crisis , just last week.

Lightspeed Studios posted the following message on the game’s website , announcing the game’s end-of-service.

Thank you for your support for FINAL FANTASY XIV MOBILE (Chinese Edition) as always.

On the launch day of FINAL FANTASY XIV MOBILE (Chinese Edition) in the Chinese mainland, we said, “Resonate with the Light, and blaze as one,” and promised that the adventure had just begun. Over this time, it is you who have truly brought “Mobile Eorzea” to life. Every suggestion you shared on gameplay, experiences, and features guided us to build the game world. Seasoned Warriors of Light stepped up as mentors, walking newcomers through the confusion of their first steps into Eorzea. And countless mornings and nights spent fighting side by side came together to form the vast, warm community we once envisioned together. To have resonated with all of you adventurers on this journey has been the greatest fortune of this world we built together, and a memory we will always treasure.

Today, however, we must regretfully share an important announcement. Due to adjustments in business operations and changes in the market environment, Tencent and Square Enix have, through amicable negotiation, decided to terminate the licensing agreement for FINAL FANTASY XIV MOBILE (Chinese Edition). We are sorry to announce that we will officially stop the game operation for FINAL FANTASY XIV MOBILE (Chinese Edition) in the Chinese mainland at 11:00 (UTC+8) on September 30, 2026.

To everyone who has journeyed with us until now, we sincerely apologize that we must end this service without being able to fully live up to your expectations. At the same time, we wish to express our heartfelt gratitude to all of you who have supported us along the way. Thank you, truly, from the bottom of our hearts. To everyone who has been looking forward to the global version, we also offer our sincere apologies for being unable to meet your expectations.”

Additionally, here’s the service termination schedule.

While the mobile version may be shutting down, FFXIV continues to be one of the more successful MMORPGs out there. The Nintendo Switch 2 version launches in August, and the next expansion, Evercold , is scheduled to release in January 2027.

This post may contain affiliate links. If you use these links to buy something, CGMagazine may earn a commission. However, please know this does not impact our reviews or opinions in any way. See our ethics statement and review policy .

items.title: Final Fantasy 14 Mobile’s global launch has been cancelled
items.summary: Final Fantasy 14 Mobile is being shut down in China, and a global release has been cancelled.
items.content:
Final Fantasy 14 Mobile is being shut down in China, and a global release has been cancelled.

Video Game News 17th Jul 2026 / 2:38 pm Posted by Dave Aubrey Tencent’s LightSpeed Studios is shutting down the Chinese version of Final Fantasy 14 Mobile and cancelling the planned global release.

The game launched in China in June 2025 and is now being shut down at the end of September 2026.

A Service Termination Announcement was posted to the FF14 Mobile website , and the full announcement can be read below.

Thank you for your support for FINAL FANTASY XIV MOBILE (Chinese Edition) as always.

On the launch day of FINAL FANTASY XIV MOBILE (Chinese Edition) in the Chinese mainland, we said, “Resonate with the Light, and blaze as one,” and promised that the adventure had just begun. Over this time, it is you who have truly brought “Mobile Eorzea” to life. Every suggestion you shared on gameplay, experiences, and features guided us to build the game world. Seasoned Warriors of Light stepped up as mentors, walking newcomers through the confusion of their first steps into Eorzea. And countless mornings and nights spent fighting side by side came together to form the vast, warm community we once envisioned together. To have resonated with all of you adventurers on this journey has been the greatest fortune of this world we built together, and a memory we will always treasure.

Today, however, we must regretfully share an important announcement. Due to adjustments in business operations and changes in the market environment, Tencent and Square Enix have, through amicable negotiation, decided to terminate the licensing agreement for FINAL FANTASY XIV MOBILE (Chinese Edition). We are sorry to announce that we will officially stop the game operation for FINAL FANTASY XIV MOBILE (Chinese Edition) in the Chinese mainland at 11:00 (UTC+8) on September 30, 2026.

To everyone who has journeyed with us until now, we sincerely apologize that we must end this service without being able to fully live up to your expectations. At the same time, we wish to express our heartfelt gratitude to all of you who have supported us along the way. Thank you, truly, from the bottom of our hearts. To everyone who has been looking forward to the global version, we also offer our sincere apologies for being unable to meet your expectations.

Except as otherwise required by laws and regulations, or otherwise agreed in the agreement between Tencent and users, we will delete the account data, character information, and other related data of players who registered and played in the Chinese mainland, in accordance with the Personal Information Protection Law after the game servers are shut down.

There are 76 days remaining until the service ends. To accompany everyone through this final journey in FINAL FANTASY XIV MOBILE (Chinese Edition), our team will do our best. Thank you again for your support.

Rumours of FF14 Mobile first appeared in July 2024, and the game was formally announced in November 2024 .

The main Final Fantasy 14 game, meanwhile, has been enjoying success. The game will launch on Nintendo Switch 2 later this year , and the Evercold expansion is coming in January 2027.

items.title: Final Fantasy 14 Shutdown Officially Confirmed, Mark Your Calendars
items.summary: FFXIV game is shutting down soon.
items.content:
By Austin King Published Jul 17, 2026, 8:51 AM EDT Austin King is the Senior Editor of Gaming at ScreenRant and has been with the site since 2020 with a focus on RPGs and tabletop games. He previously managed content for Geek to Geek Media, and has served as a writer for sites like Nintendad, Geek Fitness, and more. In addition to his work at ScreenRant, he also hosts the Dragon Quest FM podcast and is the author of several books, including co-writing Nimbus (a #1 bestseller in 2013). His latest is the nonfiction The Dragon Quest Book, available now. He's written about pop culture, video games, and genre studies over the course of his career. You can find him on BlueSky , X , and at Dragonquestaustin.com Sign in to your ScreenRant account Add Us Summary Generate a summary of this story Like Like follow Follow followed Followed Thread 1 Log in Here is a fact-based summary of the story contents: Try something different: Show me the facts Explain it like I’m 5 Give me a lighthearted recap Final Fantasy 14 may not be in everyone's good graces at the moment after a mixed reception for Dawntrail , but the critically-acclaimed MMORPG still has a massive fanbase and plenty of accolades, especially from its Shadowbringers and Endwalkers expansions. There's even a new Switch 2 version of FFXIV on the way . So, it might be somewhat of a shock to learn one FFXIV game is shutting down completely later this year.

Shutdowns from Square Enix haven't been completely unheard of. Various mobile games, for instance, have been closed this year already with more expected by the end of 2026. That's the case here, since it's been announced that Final Fantasy 14 Mobile , co-developed with Tencent, will be shutting down forever on September 30, 2026.

FFXIV Mobile is for the Chinese mainland and hasn't been released globally yet. Now, it looks like that may never happen, since the game is closing its servers in September. " Due to adjustments in business operations and changes in the market environmen t," reads the official statement, " Tencent and Square Enix have, through amicable negotiation, decided to terminate the licensing agreement for FINAL FANTASY XIV MOBILE ."

The post goes on to list the timetable for when closures begin:

So, no new users starting today. And anyone who does play FFXIV Mobile will have 76 days to prepare for the services to be terminated.

Square Enix hasn't had great luck with its mobile games, so this isn't entirely surprising. Still, considering how popular Final Fantasy 14 is, it is a bit strange that FFXIV Mobile is closing down before it ever had a chance to launch around the world. It's not all bad news, though. The rest of FFXIV on other platforms seems alive and well.

In addition to the Switch 2 version in August, a new expansion called Evercold will release for FFXIV next year. New information has been revealed at FFXIV Fan Fest already, and future events should show off even more details regarding the new classes, zones, and raids. Unless you've been playing FFXIV Mobile , you shouldn't be affected by this latest closure for Final Fantasy 14 .

Final Fantasy 14 Mobile Like MMORPG Action Adventure Systems Developer(s) Lightspeed Studios Publisher(s) Square Enix Multiplayer Online Co-Op, Online Multiplayer Franchise Final Fantasy Number of Players 1+ 6 Images Close

items.title: Final Fantasy XIV previews Patch 7.55 arriving July 28, 2026
items.summary: Final Fantasy XIV’s next update is set for July 28, 2026 and will feature a new adventuring foray into the Occult Crescent and the capstone quests for Phantom weapon enhancement, allied societies…
items.content:
Final Fantasy XIV ‘s next update is set for July 28, 2026 and will feature a new adventuring foray into the Occult Crescent and the capstone quests for Phantom weapon enhancement, allied societies, and Hildibrand’s “inconceivable” adventures.

In the North Horn of the Occult Crescent, players will be able to explore another large zone replete with critical encounters and engagements to raise their Knowledge level and the eight new Phantom Jobs that will be added. One major difference this time around is the implementation strategy of the Forked Tower Magic dungeon, which will be tuned according to feedback that the Forked Tower Blood didn’t have a more approachable difficulty and participation requirement, amongst other issues.

We will also see the finale of Dawntrail ‘s Hildibrand sidequest saga, which brings to the fore the building conflict between the hard-boiled detective Hardiboiled and the rogue Carnelian. Players will also be able to enhance their Phantom Weapons to their final iteration and undertake a quest chain that is unlocked by completing all three of the allied society storylines in Dawntrail .

As for what’s next in the 7.5 patch series: in Patch 7.56 (September 6), we will get the conclusion of the main scenario leading to the Evercold expansion and a new beastmaster limited job. During August, the team will also be holding a beta for the Nintendo Switch 2 version of the game. Note that you will need an active Nintendo Switch Online subscription and a (discounted) Final Fantasy XIV subscription when the game launches following the beta period.

Getting caught up before the next patch? You can check out our guide to unlocking content and features in Patch 7.5 and 7.51 .

Final Fantasy XIV is now available on Windows PC, Steam, PlayStation 4, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S. A Nintendo Switch 2 version is planned for release after the conclusion of a “month-long” beta test in August 2026.

The Occult Crescent North Horn & Forked Tower: Magic

“Stranded in the heart of Shades’ Triangle with no choice but to keep exploring, Ketenramm and his crew have at last made inroads into the isle’s northern reaches.

What fate awaits the expedition on this new frontier?”

Weapon Enhancement Quests Phantom Weapon Final Upgrade

“Honed with ancient knowledge and imbued with arcane power, your phantom weapon is tantalizingly close to its final form.

Alas, Gerolt’s furrowed brow hints at hurdles yet to come…”

Sidequests Inconceivably Further Hildibrand Adventures

“Detective Hardiboiled remains furious at Hildibrand for allowing his nemesis, Carnelian, the chance to escape his righteous vengeance.

items.title: Wisdom of Nym: Speculating about the second half of Final Fantasy XIV’s Occult Crescent
items.summary: The Occult Crescent will soon come to a close in Final Fantasy XIV, and it's going to be rather important. When that patch drops in a couple weeks, it will be the last new max-level content we're getting in the game until Evercold hits. There's more MSQ and Beastmaster still on the way, but neither [...]
items.content:
The Occult Crescent will soon come to a close in Final Fantasy XIV , and it’s going to be rather important. When that patch drops in a couple weeks, it will be the last new max-level content we’re getting in the game until Evercold hits. There’s more MSQ and Beastmaster still on the way, but neither of those will be meant for characters at the cap. So what is this content actually going to look like?

The question is compounded by the fact that the Occult Crescent is already a new variant on the basic content profile assembled during the Bozjan line; it’s not smaller than the Southern Front, but it is more up-front in that regard, and the fact of the matter is that the Forked Tower didn’t quite land the way that the designers had intended. So what is the second half going to bring for us? What lessons can it take from the first half, and how is it going to tie into our last step of Phantom Weapons?

First of all, I think the likelihood that the last step of Phantom Weapons will look much like the first one is… unlikely. While I suspect that the actual play experience of running around the North Horn is going to feel pretty similar on a whole, I do not expect the last part will be another atma farm. At the same time, I also don’t think it’s going to involve the second half of the Forked Tower, in no small part because the first half wasn’t required for the South Horn.

The Forked Tower, in general, is kind of a problem. It tried to split the difference between being akin to the Baldesion Arsenal as a challenge mode and the end-of-zone dungeon engagements and wound up not quite working for either, especially because a big part of Eureka’s persistence is how many people didn’t ever engage with it during Stormblood . Trying to recreate that from the start wasn’t a great idea, but it also does cause problems with giving the story a grand sendoff.

There are two options, of course. The first is adding a major revision to both sides of the Tower to incentivize more people going to the first one and then hitting the second on the North Horn. The second is keeping the North Horn version similar to the other, but adding some other form of capstone content to the northern half.

I do think that one of the major issues that the Southern Horn had – currency – is likely to be addressed proactively on the North Horn. The addition of currency to pot rewards was a pretty important change, and it’s one I imagine will carry forward as something that is also hopefully better balanced; as it stands, the pot rewards could be frustrating with large groups hitting the events, since only a small number of people get picked to chase one. But even that was a bandage on the problem that the silver and gold rewards just hadn’t been balanced very well.

My guess is that all of this is likely to be actively corrected or at least altered when we reach the North Horn. Whether or not it’s going to hit the balance just right remains to be seen, of course.

My expectation is also that the phantom weapon step will be more akin to a steady accumulation than random drops, and presumably it’ll be balanced in the same way as prior steps with a combination of one-time quest clears followed by tomestones. This has, in fact, been a good approach for the weapons; it adds some of the legwork ahead of just acquiring tomestones, but it also usually lets you get those tomestones while doing the legwork. More importantly, it hasn’t ever felt too tedious past the initial atma grind.

If there’s anything that I’m worried about, honestly, a lot of it comes down to gearing and the Forked Tower. Part of the problem with the content in the first half was that you needed a very specific combination of jobs and a specific level of gear to make the tower even approachable. This isn’t a bad thing for something that’s meant to be challenging content, but a big part of what made Bozja’s end-of-zone macro dungeons work was the fact that you needed a couple of specific jobs but everyone else could sort of do whatever. This worked well when the game was pulling from a very large pool of people; everyone wound up getting tugged into the same pool, and the flexibility of roles meant that as long as you had a couple of tanks, you’d be fine if the rest of your randos were tanks or healers or DPS or whatever.

Phantom jobs simply don’t hit that same sweet spot. I had hoped that they would, but far from being a way to turn a given job into a different role, phantom jobs offer no real stat changes but a lot of disconnected abilities that can be useful but can also just be messy. This is a major problem in terms of how the area is designed. You can’t step into the zone on a job and expect that you can fill multiple roles by choosing your phantom job; indeed, some phantom jobs are functionally useless on various jobs because of that.

I don’t see that changing very much in the North Horn because it would require a more extensive reworking than I think we’re likely to get, but I do think it helps illustrate the problem of why we don’t usually just get an extra suite of abilities. Bozja’s system worked better than this one, and the trouble is that things like big group content requires something you can’t hope to get on a random draw.

But that also ties into the gearing going on here. The South Horn could give you a bunch of long-term gear upgrade projects, and that made sense. But the North Horn is going to be harder pressed to convince anyone to spend a long time upgrading their gear to be better at this content unless it’s particularly unapproachable otherwise, and that’s going to lead to people just going elsewhere to take on the weapon upgrades (which is likely to be an option, same as it has been up until now). How that gets balanced is an open question.

Ultimately, I am looking forward to this content and I want to see what it delivers on, and I think it ultimately is on par with what we got during Bozja; I just also think that it does have areas it falters compared to that. So I’m looking forward to the changes that can be made this time around, but I also think there are some existing problems that can’t really be fixed without an entirely different approach from the start.

Feedback, as always, is welcome in the comments down below or via mail to eliot@massivelyop.com . Yes, this week the column was delayed a bit, because there was TennoCon stuff to talk about. Next week, of course, I will be speculating about the upcoming fan festival ahead of what will be a very sleep-deprived night during the fan festival.

items.title: Final Fantasy XIV - Dancing Mad Ultimate Impressions
items.summary: James checked out the latest raid to hit FFXIV Dawntrail, Dancing Mad (Ultimate), seeing how it stacks up as we head towards the next expansion for the MMORPG.
items.content:
James Galizio Posted: Jul 1, 2026 2:30 PM ET Category: Editorials The Eorzea Prospect 0

On June 2, 2026, Final Fantasy XIV: Dawntrail's second and final Ultimate raid, Dancing Mad (Ultimate), launched for the game, and besides some jank, which was just hotfixed last week, it's fair to say that the community reception has been positive - especially with consideration to Future Rewritten (Ultimate), and its own reception.

If you aren't an Ultimate raider, there are a few things to note about Futures Rewritten. First off, the game's two previous Ultimate raids from Endwalker , Dragonsong Reprise (Ultimate) and The Omega Protocol (Ultimate), were both notable steps up in terms of mechanical complexity and overall difficulty at their release. The latter, perhaps, to an overdone degree. The one-two-three punch of DSR, to Abyssos, to TOP was enough to burn many raiders out of the game, and I can testify that was very much the case for many raiders I've known firsthand.

FRU, then, was an easier encounter. With one or two exceptions, mechanics were easier. Phase 2, in particular, might as well be a joke by Ultimate standards. Mitigation and healing checks, similarly, weren't very tough - the roughest tankbusters in the fight were, arguably, in the first phase. The fight was fun, and I would never say it was an easy fight - it wasn't, and isn't - but it's very fair to say that at least some raiders felt it was an overcorrection.

The biggest issue with the fight was, and remains, downtime. Downtime mechanics were a big reason why Pictomancer was such a powerhouse, as players could prep their bursts for once the boss became targetable again to a degree that no other class was capable of. The idea of downtime mechanics is ideally to make mechanics difficult enough that players wouldn't be able to focus on both doing the mechanic while dealing damage, and yet that wasn't exactly how things turned out for FRU.

Mad (Ultimate) is different. There is no downtime, and the mechanics aren't just a tad tougher than in FRU; they're more punishing. Chances are, even if you have no intention of challenging the fight yourself, you've heard about the "fake out" ending. FRU also had a puzzle gating the final phase, but the actual mechanics behind it amounted to "don't stand in one specific, static location on the arena".

Phases 1 and 2 are far more engaging than their counterparts in FRU. Phases 3 and 4 are far more challenging. Healing and mitigation checks are more technically demanding, not just in terms of the challenge of keeping a team topped up, but with mechanics that task players with carefully healing up players at a staggered pace - all the while doing mechanics. Dancing Mad is a very tough fight, tougher than its contemporary, but the difficulty is managed in a far healthier manner.

You could argue that the fight lacks spectacle, but frankly - who cares? Not every Ultimate raid needs to be the same thing, which is where this one excels - and exactly why it feels like the perfect cherry on top for an expansion that, if nothing else, has been excellent for raiders whenever it took the chance to think outside the box. With Final Fantasy XIV's future looking so promising, it feels great to bow out of Dawntrail on such a high note.

In this weekly column, Jatobi takes a look at the world of FFXIV and the many things going on in the endless adventure.

James Galizio has been writing about gaming and technology from LA since 2014, with bylines at outlets such as Game Informer, Kotaku and RPG Site. He plays way too much Final Fantasy XIV, but you can also usually find him playing the latest RPG out of Japan. He loves to spoil his cat, Mishy.

items.title: Wisdom of Nym: Why do so many of Final Fantasy XIV’s base jobs struggle with identity?
items.summary: As Evercold approaches for Final Fantasy XIV, I hope that everyone is ready to have a new version of Monk, Dragoon, Bard, and Summoner. At the very least. When I speculated on the jobs I expect to be the most different in Evolved mode, those were all in the crosshairs, and they're not really alone. Three of those [...]
items.content:
As Evercold approaches for Final Fantasy XIV , I hope that everyone is ready to have a new version of Monk, Dragoon, Bard, and Summoner. At the very least. When I speculated on the jobs I expect to be the most different in Evolved mode, those were all in the crosshairs, and they’re not really alone. Three of those jobs are pretty explicitly the “we’re still trying to figure out the job while the game is in motion” poster children. I would be more surprised if an expansion didn’t have a big revision for Monk at this point.

This is interesting to me because we’re also talking about the most core jobs to the game as a whole. The jobs that were available in the game’s initial relaunch have seemingly gone through the largest number of revisions, and you might say, “Well, they’ve been around the longest,” but that’s in contrast to most other jobs. Red Mage and Samurai haven’t ever had a real fundamental redesign to the extent of the older jobs, and Stormblood was back in 2017. And what makes it even more compelling to me is that the usual explanation that would track is not true here: This is not a different team.

For better or for worse, it’s not exactly new to observe that when a new team comes on to an MMORPG, they’re likely to redesign parts of the game to be closer to their vision. Considering that Final Fantasy XIV’s relaunch is now more than a decade in the rearview, I think it would make sense if there were people who came to the game with a preference for older versions of the game. You can really feel the influence of the new design leads and game director.

Except that’s not actually true, which is why I made the joke about the new director. The game doesn’t have a new director; it has the same director, who has been the director for the game’s entire relaunch now. That’s what makes it particularly interesting. This isn’t a matter of new staff coming in with different ideas but the same people changing their minds about what they want from these jobs time and again.

Why is that? You’d expect things like Monk and Dragoon to be some of the most fundamental components of the game’s identity. And the thing is that I think they kind of are, but they’re also victims of the problem of how to manifest the entire franchise.

Let’s take a step back and look at Monk. I mean, really, let’s look at Monk through the ages because Monk is one of the most quintessentially Final Fantasy jobs possible. It has been in the game since the first installment. Every game with a job system has it. Almost every single game that doesn’t have jobs has someone who is clearly a Monk (Josef, Yang, Sabin, Tifa, Zell, Amarant, Snow), and the ones where there isn’t a clear Monk in your party still have someone with clear parallels or who just require the smallest bit of squinting. Monk is always a thing.

So what makes a Monk unique? What unifies all of these characters? Therein lies something of a problem because the answer is… actually kinda vague beyond “they all punch stuff.” Monk’s utility and worth varies a lot between games.

FFXIV has always had Pugilist and then Monk, and the idea of “punching” has been part of the game since the beginning. Yet at the same time, it has a lot of ground to cover there which other jobs don’t, and it is a more amorphous concept just from the word go. The early concept for Monk at launch was very much about a job that had something of a perpetual combo going, weaving between its weapon skills in and out of stances instead of along a specific set of combos.

The problem, of course, was that this meant Monk suffered a lot during periods when the boss was impossible to target. So skills were added to solve that problem, but also that caused other issues with Monk rotations. Monk has always been assembled from individual parts that make sense and feel Monk-like, but Monk as an idea in this franchise has a lot of plasticity.

By contrast, Red Mage has a clearer focus here. Red Mage, since its creation, has been a job about balancing spellcasting and building to a melee combo. That is what Red Mage is . It was designed, first and foremost, to fit into a specific design and function according to that, and any changes are gauged against how well they balance against that basic design and function.

Lots of the things Monk was designed to do in ARR have slowly become not a vital component of the game over time. Dragoon having increasingly long combos became a problem as any slip meant that Dragoon had to start from zero all over again. Summoner, as a concept, has gone through three different variants of “how do we summon Bahamut” because that is traditionally the iconic Big Summon of the franchise. But it’s hard to keep adding new stuff when you’ve already added Bahamut to the game.

Last but not least, a lot of the changes have also come into play as new jobs have entered the mix and changed the game. Reaper, for example, is in many ways modeled after Dark Knight from Final Fantasy XI , specifically using a scythe instead of a greatsword. I mentioned how Viper was able to take a lot of the “Rogue as damage-dealer” ideas that no longer fit in Ninja, leaving Ninja to be more focused on Ninja Stuff. As these new jobs create a new focus, old jobs can adopt different identities in turn, even if that changes the identity of what those jobs used to be.

Oh, and let’s not forget that the initial job designs were also built around the idea that a single class would become multiple different jobs, something that did not work. Classes being completely deprecated is one of those elements that the designers just were not building for in 2013.

In summary, there are a lot of factors that have led to these jobs just steadily changing more and more over time, until their present forms are largely new identities from the original. Paladin cares about MP in a way it never did in the base game. Monk currently plays more like a support version of Viper than anything. These things have changed, in many cases significantly.

items.title: Breaking Brick Mountains Returns on June 25!
items.summary: The collaboration event with Dragon Quest X, Breaking Brick Mountains, returns Thursday, June 25! Battle against gargantuan golems from the Dragon Quest series, once again making their way to Eorzea! Don't miss this opportunity to earn rich
items.content:
The collaboration event with Dragon Quest X, Breaking Brick Mountains, returns Thursday, June 25! Battle against gargantuan golems from the Dragon Quest series, once again making their way to Eorzea! Don't miss this opportunity to earn rich rewards, including the king slime crown, thug's mug, and the newly added Dragon Quest X framer's kit. * This event is identical to the one previously held. * If you have already completed the event, you can replay the quests via the seasonal event replay feature. For details, please check the special site.

Event Duration

Thursday, June 25, 2026 at 1:00 a.m. (PDT) / 8:00 (GMT) / 9:00 (BST) / 18:00 (AEST) to Monday, July 13, 2026 at 7:59 a.m. (PDT) / 14:59 (GMT) / 15:59 (BST) /14 July at 0:59 (AEST)

How to Participate

Begin the quest "Breaking Brick Mountains" by speaking to Havak Alvak of the Mythril Eye in Ul'dah – Steps of Nald (X:12.1 Y:8.2). During this event, players can purchase a King Slime Crown by speaking with the Toughie in Ul'dah – Steps of Nald (X:8.2 Y:12.2).

Rewards

Visit the special site for more details.

items.title: Wisdom of Nym: Speculating on Final Fantasy XIV Evercold’s weekly activity system
items.summary: So one of the very specific things I said when I was talking about Final Fantasy XIV's last fan festival was cautioning people about getting out over their skis regarding the weekly activity system that's coming in Evercold. We currently know very, very little about it from the base level, and thus it is a bad idea [...]
items.content:
So one of the very specific things I said when I was talking about Final Fantasy XIV’s last fan festival was cautioning people about getting out over their skis regarding the weekly activity system that’s coming in Evercold . We currently know very, very little about it from the base level, and thus it is a bad idea to speak too confidently about any of it because… we know very little about it. Presumably we’ll learn more in July. But here I am, writing a column in which I am knowingly getting out over my skis a bit.

In part, this is a deliberate maneuver because I am writing this as much as anything to demonstrate the known unknowns. But it’s also because I feel like doing a bit of speculating and seeing how close to the intent I actually manage to hit. So let’s talk about it, and the first thing I’m going to do is say that the most obvious source we have for the activity system is not something we should actually use as a source.

One of the slides from Yoshida’s keynote specifically shows off a page full of weekly activities as a mockup, and this is the most obvious source for speculating about what the weekly system will look like. However, I am honestly pretty certain that it was never meant to be anything more than a mockup to show what the interface will look like, rather than any sort of actual sample. For example, one of its items is “Embark on an Ocean Fishing voyage,” and while that one is cut off at the bottom of the shot, we can see that it’s offering as many points as an instanced dungeon.

So if you’re using that image as your basis on values or expectations, you should not be. It is not accurate and honestly does not pretend that it is showing you anything beyond a mockup. It’s why it was shown once and then not again. It is a demonstration of what the interface will look like, not what it will be or what it will reward, and thus nothing that is on there should be taken in absolute terms.

Where, then, do we start? Well, I would say that the smartest place to start is actually with our current system because that should give us some kind of baseline.

As it currently stands, players can earn 450 time-limited tomestones per week. If we assume that the players doing so are also earning weekly rewards as they are relevant elsewhere (and we pretend that you can wear two rings from the tomestone vendor, which you can’t, but bear with me), it takes a little over 12 weeks to get a full set of gear including your weapons from zero. So let’s make 12 weeks a baseline. This works pretty nicely, as it happens; there are 12 slots for you to equip something, after all, even if only one combat job reliably puts anything in the offhand slot, so it feels broadly “right” in this sense.

This broadly corresponds to the larger interface mockup, which features its milestones at increments of 3000 from what we can see. And I would go so far as to venture that we can expect, on a weekly basis, for the same basic rules to apply as they currently do, so five Expert dungeons in a week will get you to that milestone.

Keep in mind that at this point, we do not know what “expert” dungeons will look like if, indeed, they even still exist or use that name. Again, this is strictly about the basic shapes, and we haven’t been told that content itself is wildly changing so much as the ways in which we get rewards from it will change.

All of this is based on the assumption that our expected weekly activities will look the same as they do now if that’s how we choose to play. But it’s also assuming that our expected weekly activities are also confined to top-level play, and it’s here that I think there’s the possibility for something more interesting to go on. I just mentioned that we’re talking about “five Expert dungeons will cap you on rewards,” but there are two possible implementations here. The first – if point values stay the same – would be that “clear an Expert dungeon” is worth 600 points and can be repeated. But the second is that “clear an Expert dungeon,” “clear an instanced dungeon,” and “clear a max-level dungeon” are all individual objectives – or all of the aforementioned along with “clear a roulette” and “clear content with random party members.”

The thing is, doing an Expert dungeon would count for all of those activities. But it’s very possible for other things to count for several as well. If you’re doing leveling dungeons on other jobs, you’re still clearing instanced dungeons! And I suspect that’s part of the point.

While I can’t be certain at this time, my suspicion is that stacking different objectives is part of the end goal with this new system, but also in a way that doesn’t force you to choose quite as often. As it stands now, committing to level up a new job means that you have to do a lot of content on top of what you also will be doing in terms of endgame maintenance. Not an onerous burden by any means, but it does mean that if you have less time you have to choose between leveling or getting tomes.

I’m hopeful, albeit not certain, that one of the goals with weekly activity as a system is to change that. That’s not to say that it’ll be faster to level up another job than to just do Expert roulette, as I posited before; rather, it’s that if you’re already doing another job, you can get something out of it in terms of gear progress as well. You don’t have to do as many dungeons in a week to cap out your gear progression because it’s your overall activity that matters most.

That is an exciting prospect to me. As someone who generally engages with the game very broadly, I am fond of feeling like all of it feeds together more than it currently does. In some ways, it’s a mirror of something I’ve already expressed joy over when it comes to the game’s flexibility in its endgame. It is something that could use a refresh, and I’m hopeful to see something that promotes a healthy engagement with the game as a whole instead of a strict regimen – something that I think is broadly within the realm of possibility, obviously.

Or maybe it will just be “you can do your routine of experts all at once instead” and nothing will really change. I’ve been wrong before!

items.title: FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE is available for pre-purchase now!
items.summary: https://store.steampowered.com/app/3259780/ What if FINAL FANTASY had continued to push the limits of pixel art and evolved from its classic roots? The first FINAL FANTASY to join the HD‑2D lineup, FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE delivers a visuall
items.content:
https://store.steampowered.com/app/3259780/ What if FINAL FANTASY had continued to push the limits of pixel art and evolved from its classic roots? The first FINAL FANTASY to join the HD‑2D lineup, FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE delivers a visually striking experience that feels both nostalgic and new, enhanced by dynamic camera angles and pixelized 3D models that make every moment more dramatic and immersive. Return to the pixel‑art world of FINAL FANTASY, evolved from its classic roots. Travel with companions bound by fate and call on Visions to aid you in battle to uncover the truth behind a force that threatens to bring everything to ruin.

Digital Deluxe Edition:

This product is a bundle that includes the FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE base game along with the Magitek & Grimoire Deluxe Pack, which contains an item that transforms your chocobo's appearance into magitek armor, an item exclusive to this pack that unlocks a certain character's Fel Meteor Limit Burst, and useful items to aid you at the start of your journey. Digital Deluxe Edition Contents: ▼ FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE Base Game ▼ Magitek & Grimoire Deluxe Pack *Images are for illustrative purposes only. Actual in‑game visuals may differ. 　 Pack Contents: 　 • Magitek Armor Key x1 　 • Archwitch’s Grimoire x1 　 • Mastery Ring x1 (Increases MRP gained in battle by a certain amount) 　 • Tent x5 　 • Mist Potion x5 　 • Mist Ether x5 　 • Phoenix Down x5 　 • Remedy x3

Pre-order Bonus: Magitek Airship & Squire Starter Pack

*Images are for illustrative purposes only. Actual in‑game visuals may differ. This set contains an item that transforms your airship’s appearance into a magitek vessel, along with useful items to aid you at the start of your journey. Pack Contents: • Magitek Airship Passkey x1 • Knight’s Greatsword x1 • Chestplate of Preparation x1 (Increases EXP gained in battle by a certain amount) • Magicite Shard x1 • Tent x3 • Mist Potion x3

items.title: Patch 7.51 Notes
items.summary: Patch 7.51 introduces the seventh installment of the ultimate raid series, Dancing Mad (Ultimate), as well as new custom deliveries featuring Tiisol Ja. And for our intrepid Disciples of the Hand and Land, new exploration awaits on Auxesia.
items.content:
Patch 7.51 introduces the seventh installment of the ultimate raid series, Dancing Mad (Ultimate), as well as new custom deliveries featuring Tiisol Ja. And for our intrepid Disciples of the Hand and Land, new exploration awaits on Auxesia. * Additional features will be implemented in future updates:
  • The Occult Crescent: North Horn, Weapon Enhancement Quests - Phantom Weapons, Inconceivably Further Hildibrand Adventures sidequests, and Allied Society capstone sidequests are scheduled to release in Patch 7.55.
  • Further main scenario quests and the beastmaster limited job are scheduled to release in Patch 7.56.
Read the full patch notes on the Lodestone. Launch FINAL FANTASY XIV Now

items.title: The Make It Rain Campaign Begins May 29!
items.summary: The Make It Rain Campaign returns once more to the Gold Saucer! With a 50% MGP bonus and an assortment of adorable otter-themed goods on offer, you won't want to miss out! Furthermore, campaign attendants will be offering a range of items a
items.content:
The Make It Rain Campaign returns once more to the Gold Saucer! With a 50% MGP bonus and an assortment of adorable otter-themed goods on offer, you won't want to miss out! Furthermore, campaign attendants will be offering a range of items at discounted prices, including some exclusive limited-time deals, so be sure to join the festivities and rake in the rewards while you can!

Event Duration

From Friday, May 29, 2026 at 1:00 a.m. (PDT) / 8:00 (GMT) / 9:00 (BST) / 18:00 (AEST) to Wednesday, June 24, 2026 at 7:59 a.m. (PDT) / 14:59 (GMT) / 15:59 (BST) / Thursday, 25 June at 0:59 (AEST)

How to Participate

Begin the quest "You Otter Be There" by speaking to Ollier in Ul’dah - Steps of Nald (X:9.4 Y:9.1). * Players must be level 15 and above to start the quest. * Players must first complete the quest "It Could Happen to You."

Rewards

* MGP rewards from the Gold Saucer have been increased by 50% for the duration of this event. * Certain items available during previous iterations of the event can be obtained via the campaign attendant. * The Crystal Tower striker, Monster Toss machine, Cuff-a-Cur machine, and a number of other prizes may be purchased at a reduced MGP cost from the campaign attendant. Visit the special site for more details.

items.title: Play Free for up to 4 Days in the Latest Free Login Campaign!
items.summary: In the hope that would-be adventurers can experience all that FFXIV has to offer, we are pleased to announce the latest Free Login Campaign. Follow the Trail to the Heavens ! In Patch 7.5, you can face Enuo in the Unmaking, take a stroll th
items.content:
In the hope that would-be adventurers can experience all that FFXIV has to offer, we are pleased to announce the latest Free Login Campaign. Follow the Trail to the Heavens! In Patch 7.5, you can face Enuo in the Unmaking, take a stroll through Windurst: The Third Walk in the FINAL FANTASY XI alliance raid crossover, and leap into battle in Archeia Harmonias, an all-new arena for Crystalline Conflict. A host of other additions and adjustments will make your time in Eorzea more streamlined and enjoyable, including increased furnishing placement limits, the consolidation of dyes, and additional pieces of gear that can now be converted to outfit glamour or stored in the armoire. With the introduction of Patch 7.5, the Shadowbringers expansion can now be played as part of the FINAL FANTASY XIV Starter Edition. The expansion will be automatically registered to your account if you already own the Starter Edition, so why not take this opportunity to jump into the adventure?

If you or any of your friends are currently taking a break from your adventures, you won't want to miss out on this limited-time event! Visit the Free Login Campaign website for details.

items.title: The Maiden's Rhapsody Returns!
items.summary: Experience the intriguing tale bridging Hydaelyn and Vana'diel as The Maiden's Rhapsody─a past collaboration with FFXI─returns to the realm once more! Embark on a journey with one of Vana'diel's heroes and discover what twists of fate broug
items.content:
Experience the intriguing tale bridging Hydaelyn and Vana'diel as The Maiden's Rhapsody─a past collaboration with FFXI─returns to the realm once more! Embark on a journey with one of Vana'diel's heroes and discover what twists of fate brought her to our realm. * This event is identical to the one previously held. * If you have already completed the event, you can replay the quests via the seasonal event replay feature. We highly recommend learning more about this feature on the collaboration special site.

Event Duration

From Friday, May 8, 2026 at 1:00 a.m. (PDT) / 8:00 (GMT) / 9:00 (BST) / 18:00 (AEST) to Monday, May 25, 2026 at 7:59 a.m. (PDT) / 14:59 (GMT) / 15:59 (BST) / Tuesday, 26 May at 00:59 (AEST)

How to Participate

Begin the quest "A Journey to Remember" by speaking to the Remumu in Limsa Lominsa - Upper Decks (X:11.6 Y:11.2).

Rewards

Visit the special site for more details.
